questions.specification:
A researcher collects data on the hours spent practicing a musical instrument, $m$, and the performance score, $p$, out of 100, for eight students.

| $m$ (hours) | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | 12 | 14 | 16 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $p$ (score) | 50 | 60 | 65 | 70 | 75 | 80 | 85 | 90 |

Let $L_{1}$ be the regression line of $m$ on $p$, given by $m=a p+b$. Let $L_{2}$ be the regression line of $p$ on $m$.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Calculating the linear relationship between the data in the form y=ax+b
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) and the mention of L2 (regression of p on m) involve forming a regression line equation of the form y = ax + b (even if not fully solved in all parts).

parts.content: Find the values of $a$ and $b$.
parts.markscheme:
$\bar{p}=\frac{50+60+65+70+75+80+85+90}{8}=71.875, \bar{m}=\frac{2+4+6+8+10+12+14+16}{8}=9$

$a=\frac{\sum\left(p_{i}-\bar{p}\right)\left(m_{i}-\bar{m}\right)}{\sum\left(p_{i}-\bar{p}\right)^{2}} \approx 0.365$

$b=\bar{m}-a \bar{p} \approx 9-0.365 \times 71.875 \approx -17.2$

$a \approx 0.365, b \approx -17.2$
<marks>A1</marks>
<marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify variables
steps.content:
The question asks for the regression line of **$m$ on $p$**, given by the equation $$m = ap + b$$ comparing this to the standard linear equation $$y = ax + b$$, we can identify our variables:

- The independent variable ($x$) is the score, $p$.
- The dependent variable ($y$) is the hours, $m$.

It is critical to input these into the correct lists in your calculator.

steps.title: State the values
steps.content:
The calculator output gives:

$$a ≈ 0.36491...$$
$$b ≈ -17.228...$$

Round these values to 3 significant figures as per IB standards.

$$a = 0.365$$
$$b = -17.2$$

steps.title: Identify regression variables
steps.content:
The question asks for the regression line of $m$ on $p$, given by the equation $m = ap + b$. This implies:
- $m$ is the dependent variable (acting as $y$).
- $p$ is the independent variable (acting as $x$).

We need to determine the gradient $a$ and the $y$-intercept $b$.

steps.title: Calculate means
steps.content:
First, calculate the mean of the scores ($\,\bar{p}\,)$ and the mean of the hours ($\,\bar{m}\,)$:

$$\bar{p} = \frac{\sum p}{n} = \frac{50+60+65+70+75+80+85+90}{8} = \frac{575}{8} = 71.875$$

$$\bar{m} = \frac{\sum m}{n} = \frac{2+4+6+8+10+12+14+16}{8} = \frac{72}{8} = 9$$

calculator.description: Calculate mean of p and m

steps.title: Calculate sum of squares and products
steps.content:
Next, calculate the sum of squared deviations for $p$ (denoted $S_{pp}$) and the sum of the products of deviations (covariance term $S_{pm}$):

$$ S_{pp} = \sum (p_i - \bar{p})^2 = (50-71.875)^2 + \dots + (90-71.875)^2 = 1246.875 $$

$$ S_{pm} = \sum (p_i - \bar{p})(m_i - \bar{m}) = (50-71.875)(2-9) + \dots = 455 $$

gdc.instructions: Use spreadsheet/statistics mode

ti84.instructions: Enter sums into lists L1(m) and L2(p), then 2-Var Stats to find sum of squares

desmos.instructions: Calculate total variance/covariance sums

steps.title: Calculate gradient a
steps.content:
Using the least squares regression formula for the gradient $a$ of $m$ on $p$:

$$ a = \frac{S_{pm}}{S_{pp}} = \frac{\sum (p_i - \bar{p})(m_i - \bar{m})}{\sum (p_i - \bar{p})^2} $$

Substituting our values:
$$ a = \frac{455}{1246.875} \approx 0.36491... $$

Rounding to 3 significant figures:
$$ a \approx 0.365 $$

steps.title: Calculate intercept b
steps.content:
Now, find the $y$-intercept $b$ using the property that the regression line passes through the mean point $(\bar{p}, \bar{m})$:

$$ b = \bar{m} - a\bar{p} $$

$$ b = 9 - (0.36491...)(71.875) $$

$$ b \approx 9 - 26.228... \approx -17.228... $$

Rounding to 3 significant figures:
$$ b \approx -17.2 $$

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The values for the regression line $m = ap + b$ are:

$$ a \approx 0.365 $$
$$ b \approx -17.2 $$

steps.title: Identify the intersection property
steps.content:
The question states that the two regression lines intersect at the mean point $(\bar{m}, \bar{p})$. This is a fundamental property of linear regression: both the line of $x$ on $y$ and $y$ on $x$ pass through the common mean point $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$.

To find the intersection, we simply need to calculate the mean of the $m$ values ($\bar{m}$) and the mean of the $p$ values ($\bar{p}$).

parts.content: A student practiced for 9 hours. Estimate their performance score using an appropriate regression line.
parts.markscheme:
Evidence of using $L_{2}$ ($p$ on $m$):

$p \approx 2.71 m + 47.5$ <marks>M1</marks>

Substitute $m=9$:

$p = 2.71(9) + 47.5 \approx 71.9$ (accept 71.875)
<marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the correct regression line
steps.content:
The question asks us to estimate the **performance score** ($p$) given the **hours practiced** ($m$). 

Since we are predicting $p$ from a known value of $m$, we must use the regression line of $p$ on $m$ (labeled as $L_2$ in the question). This minimizes the vertical errors in the $p$ values.

highlights.text: regression line of p on m

steps.title: Enter data into GDC
steps.content:
Enter the given data into your graphing calculator. Set the hours $m$ as your $x$-variable (List 1) and the scores $p$ as your $y$-variable (List 2).

$$m: \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16\}$$
$$p: \{50, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80, 85, 90\}$$

steps.title: Calculate regression equation
steps.content:
Perform a linear regression of the form $y = ax + b$ (or $p = am + b$).

The calculator gives:
*   Gradient $a \approx 2.71$ ($2.7083...$)
*   y-intercept $b = 47.5$

So, the equation for $L_2$ is:
$$p \approx 2.71m + 47.5$$

gdc.instructions: Select [CALC] > [REG] > [x] (Linear Regression). Ensure X is List 1 and Y is List 2.

ti84.instructions: Press [STAT] > [CALC] > [4:LinReg(ax+b)]. Ensure Xlist is L1 and Ylist is L2. Press Calculate.

desmos.instructions: Type the regression equation: y_1 ~ mx_1 + c

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, substitute the value $m = 9$ hours into our equation to estimate the score.

Using the rounded values:
$$p = 2.71(9) + 47.5$$

$$p = 24.39 + 47.5$$

$$p = 71.89$$

(Note: If you use the exact mean values, you get exactly $71.875$.)

desmos.instructions: Calculate 2.71 * 9 + 47.5

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The estimated performance score is approximately **71.9** (or 71.875).

$$p \approx 71.9$$

steps.title: Calculate mean practice hours
steps.content:
First, let's calculate the mean number of practice hours, denoted as $\bar{m}$.

We sum the hours and divide by the number of students ($n=8$):

$$\bar{m} = \frac{2+4+6+8+10+12+14+16}{8} = \frac{72}{8} = 9$$

Notice that the question asks us to estimate the score for exactly **9 hours**.

ti84.instructions: Enter data into L1. Press [STAT] > CALC > 1-Var Stats L1. Read x̄.

calculator.description: Calculate the mean of the list of practice hours

steps.title: Apply Mean Point Property
steps.content:
A fundamental property of regression lines is that they **always pass through the mean point** $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$.

Since the regression line $L_2$ (score $p$ on hours $m$) passes through $(\bar{m}, \bar{p})$, and the input value $m=9$ is exactly equal to $\bar{m}$, the estimated score must be exactly the mean score $\bar{p}$.

$$ \text{If } m = \bar{m}, \text{ then } p = \bar{p} $$

steps.title: Calculate mean score
steps.content:
Now we simply calculate the mean performance score, $\bar{p}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \bar{p} &= \frac{50+60+65+70+75+80+85+90}{8} \\ &= \frac{575}{8} \\ &= 71.875 \end{aligned}$$

steps.content:
The estimated performance score is the mean score.

$$p = 71.875$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures gives $71.9$.

questions.specification:
A café records the daily temperature, $d\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$, and the number of coffees sold, $c$, over seven days.

| $d\,(^\circ\mathrm{C})$ | 15 | 18 | 20 | 22 | 25 | 28 | 30 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $c$ | 60 | 55 | 50 | 45 | 40 | 35 | 30 |

Let $L_{1}$ be the regression line of $d$ on $c$, given by $d=k c+m$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Statistics & Probability > SL 4.10—X on y regression line > Question Type 1: Finding regression x onto y instead of y onto x
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Finding regression x onto y instead of y onto x
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks for the regression line of d on c (x on y), i.e., d = kc + m, and then uses it to estimate temperature for a given number of coffees.

parts.content: Find the values of $k$ and $m$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the value of $k$: $k = -0.5$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State the value of $m$: $m = 45.1$ ($45.0714\dots$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify variables
steps.content:
The question asks for the **regression line of $d$ on $c$** given by the equation $d = k c + m$. In the standard linear equation form $y = ax + b$:

*   The dependent variable ($y$) is the subject of the formula: **Temperature** ($d$).
*   The independent variable ($x$) is the other variable: **Coffees sold** ($c$).

Therefore, we will map $c$ to the $x$-list and $d$ to the $y$-list on our calculator.

steps.title: Enter data into GDC
steps.content:
Enter the given data into two lists on your Graphics Display Calculator (GDC). Ensure the pairs match exactly as given in the table.

*   **List 1 ($x$ - Coffees $c$):** $60, 55, 50, 45, 40, 35, 30$
*   **List 2 ($y$ - Temperature $d$):** $15, 18, 20, 22, 25, 28, 30$

steps.title: Perform linear regression
steps.content:
Run the Linear Regression calculation ($ax+b$). The calculator will determine the constants $a$ (gradient) and $b$ (y-intercept) that best fit the data.

In our equation $d = k c + m$:
*   The slope $k$ corresponds to $a$.
*   The y-intercept $m$ corresponds to $b$.

steps.title: Identify the variables
steps.content:
The question asks for the **regression line of $d$ on $c$**. This phrasing tells us which variable is independent ($x$) and which is dependent ($y$).

*   The "on $c$" part means $c$ (number of coffees) is our independent variable ($x$).
*   The "of $d$" part means $d$ (temperature) is our dependent variable ($y$).

We need to find the linear equation in the form $$d = k c + m$$

steps.title: Write the regression equation
steps.content:
Using the calculator output, we find the coefficients for the line $d = kc + m$:

*   Gradient ($k$): $-0.5$
*   Intercept ($m$): $\approx 45.0714$

So, our regression equation is:
$$d = -0.5c + 45.1$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
The question asks to estimate the temperature when **42 coffees** are sold. Substitute $c = 42$ into your regression equation.

$$d = -0.5(42) + 45.0714$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Calculate the value for $d$:

$$d = -21 + 45.0714 = 24.0714\dots$$ 

Rounding to 3 significant figures, the estimated temperature is:

$$d = 24.1\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

steps.title: Predict y at x = 10
steps.content:
Substitute $x = 10$ into the regression equation to find the predicted value $\hat{y}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \hat{y} &= -1.1318(10) + 31.061 \\ &= -11.318 + 31.061 \\ &= 19.743 \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 4 significant figures as per the markscheme:

$$\hat{y} \approx 19.74$$

steps.title: Define residual
steps.content:
The **residual** is defined as the difference between the observed value ($y$) and the predicted value ($\hat{y}$) for a specific data point.

$$\text{Residual} = y_{\text{observed}} - y_{\text{predicted}}$$ 

We need to find the residual for the point $(7, 21)$.

steps.title: Calculate prediction at x = 7
steps.content:
First, calculate the predicted value $\hat{y}$ when $x = 7$ by substituting into the regression equation.

$$\begin{aligned} \hat{y}(7) &= -1.1318(7) + 31.061 \\ &= -7.9226 + 31.061 \\ &= 23.1384 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the residual
steps.content:
Now, subtract the predicted value from the observed value ($y=21$) to find the residual.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Residual} &= 21 - 23.1384 \\ &= -2.1384 \\ &\approx -2.14 \end{aligned}$$

(More precisely: $x = -0.850192y + 26.7478$)

$$y \approx -1.13x + 31.1$$
(More precisely: $y = -1.13182x + 31.0614$)

steps.title: Estimate using x on y
steps.content:
To find the estimated value of $x$ when $y=16$, we substitute $y=16$ directly into the $x$ on $y$ equation ($x = cy + d$). This is the standard method for predicting $x$ from a given $y$ value.

$$\begin{aligned} x &= -0.850192(16) + 26.7478 \\ x &= -13.6031 + 26.7478 \\ x &\approx 13.1 \end{aligned}$$

Our first estimate is **13.1** (to 3 s.f.).

gdc.instructions: Type the expression to calculate x.

ti84.instructions: Type the expression to calculate x.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to calculate x.

steps.title: Estimate by inverting y on x
steps.content:
Next, we use the $y$ on $x$ equation ($y = ax + b$). We substitute $y=16$ and algebraically rearrange the equation to solve for $x$.

Substitute $y=16$ into $y = -1.13182x + 31.0614$:
$$16 = -1.13182x + 31.0614$$

Rearrange to make $x$ the subject:
$$\begin{aligned} 1.13182x &= 31.0614 - 16 \\ 1.13182x &= 15.0614 \\ x &= \frac{15.0614}{1.13182} \\ x &\approx 13.3 \end{aligned}$$

Our second estimate is **13.3** (to 3 s.f.).

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value of x.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value of x.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the value of x.

steps.title: Explain the difference
steps.content:
The two estimates ($13.1$ and $13.3$) are different because the two regression lines are calculated by minimizing different errors:

1. The **$x$ on $y$** line minimizes the sum of squared **horizontal** residuals (errors in $x$). This is the correct line for predicting $x$.
2. The **$y$ on $x$** line minimizes the sum of squared **vertical** residuals (errors in $y$).

The two lines are only identical (inverses of each other) if the correlation is perfect ($r = \pm 1$). In this case, $r \approx -0.98$, so the lines are distinct.

parts.content: Find the mean time spent on homework, $\bar{t}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to solve at mean point $(\bar{t}, \bar{s})$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute $t=0.15s-4.5$ into $s=5t+40$: <marks>M1</marks>

- $s=5(0.15s-4.5)+40 \Rightarrow s=0.75s-22.5+40 \Rightarrow s=0.75s+17.5$

- $0.25s=17.5 \Rightarrow s=70$

- $t=0.15(70)-4.5=10.5-4.5=6$

- $\bar{t}=6$ hours <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the intersection property
steps.content:
A key property of regression lines is that they both pass through the mean point $(\bar{t}, \bar{s})$. This means we can find the mean values by solving the two regression equations simultaneously as a system of linear equations.

The question gives us two equations:

$$\begin{aligned} (1) \quad s &= 5t + 40 \\ (2) \quad t &= 0.15s - 4.5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute t into the s equation
steps.content:
We use the **Algebraic Substitution** method. Since the second equation already expresses $t$ in terms of $s$, we substitute $t = 0.15s - 4.5$ into the first equation ($s = 5t + 40$).

$$s = 5(0.15s - 4.5) + 40$$

steps.title: Solve for s
steps.content:
Now, we expand and simplify the equation to solve for $s$ (which represents the mean score, $\bar{s}$).

$$\begin{aligned} s &= 5(0.15s) - 5(4.5) + 40 \\ s &= 0.75s - 22.5 + 40 \\ s &= 0.75s + 17.5 \end{aligned}$$

Rearranging to isolate $s$:

$$\begin{aligned} s - 0.75s &= 17.5 \\ 0.25s &= 17.5 \\ s &= \dfrac{17.5}{0.25} \\ s &= 70 \end{aligned}$$

So, the mean score $\bar{s} = 70$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 17.5 / 0.25

ti84.instructions: Calculate 17.5 / 0.25

desmos.instructions: Calculate 17.5 / 0.25

steps.title: Calculate the mean time
steps.content:
Now that we have $\bar{s} = 70$, substitute this value back into the second equation to find the mean time $\bar{t}$.

$$\begin{aligned} t &= 0.15(70) - 4.5 \\ t &= 10.5 - 4.5 \\ t &= 6 \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the mean time spent on homework is **6 hours**.

ti84.instructions: Calculate 0.15 * 70 - 4.5

steps.title: Verify with calculator
steps.content:
You can verify this system of equations using your calculator's equation solver or graphing tool.

Graph both lines:
1. $y = 5x + 40$ (where $y$ is $s$ and $x$ is $t$)
2. $x = 0.15y - 4.5 \Rightarrow y = \dfrac{x + 4.5}{0.15}$

Find their intersection point. The coordinates will correspond to $(\bar{t}, \bar{s}) = (6, 70)$.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The mean time spent on homework is:

$$\bar{t} = 6$$

steps.title: Recall the intersection property
steps.content:
The two regression lines (the line of $s$ on $t$ and the line of $t$ on $s$) always intersect at the mean point $(\bar{t}, \bar{s})$.

Therefore, to find $\bar{t}$, we need to find the coordinates where the two given lines intersect.

steps.title: Define variables for GDC
steps.content:
To use the graphic display calculator (GDC), we map the variables to $x$ and $y$:
- Let $t = x$ (independent variable for the first line)
- Let $s = y$ (dependent variable for the first line)

We need to find the $x$-coordinate of the intersection.

steps.title: Rearrange equations for graphing
steps.content:
The first equation is already in the form $y = mx + c$:

$$s = 5t + 40 \implies y = 5x + 40$$

The second equation is given as $t$ in terms of $s$. To enter it into a graphing tool, we rearrange it to express $y$ in terms of $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} t &= 0.15s - 4.5 \\ x &= 0.15y - 4.5 \\ x + 4.5 &= 0.15y \\ y &= \dfrac{x + 4.5}{0.15} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find intersection using GDC

Enter both equations into your GDC and find the intersection point.

- **Y1**: $5x + 40$
- **Y2**: $\dfrac{x + 4.5}{0.15}$

The calculator will give the intersection point $(x, y)$. The value of $x$ corresponds to the mean time $\bar{t}$.

methods.label: Using Regression Line of t on s

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
We are given a student's test score, $s = 72$, and asked to estimate their homework time, $t$. We have two regression equations available:
1. The regression line of $s$ on $t$: $s=5t+40$
2. The regression line of $t$ on $s$: $t=0.15s-4.5$

steps.title: Select the correct regression line
steps.content:
In statistics, when you want to estimate a variable $Y$ based on a known value of $X$, you must use the regression line of **$Y$ on $X$**. This line is specifically calculated to minimize the error in estimating $Y$.

Since we want to estimate $t$ given a value of $s$, we must use the **regression line of $t$ on $s$**.

$$t = 0.15s - 4.5$$

steps.title: Substitute the value
steps.content:
Substitute the score $s=72$ into the selected equation to find $t$:

$$t = 0.15(72) - 4.5$$

steps.content:
Calculate the result:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.15(72) &= 10.8 \\ t &= 10.8 - 4.5 \\ t &= 6.3 \end{aligned}$$

The estimated homework time is **6.3 hours**.

steps.title: Analyze the data range
steps.content:
First, look at the scatter diagram to determine the range of the given data for time spent on homework ($t$).

The data points (the crosses) are distributed along the horizontal axis roughly between $t=1$ and $t=10$. This defines the **range** of our observed data.

steps.title: Compare the value to the range
steps.content:
The question asks us to predict a score for a student who spent **12 hours** on homework.

We compare this value to our data range:
$$12 > 10$$

The value $t=12$ lies outside the range of the data points shown in the scatter diagram.

steps.title: Identify as extrapolation
steps.content:
When we use a regression line to predict a value outside the range of the original data, this is called **extrapolation**.

(In contrast, predicting within the range is called *interpolation*.)

steps.title: Comment on validity
steps.content:
To get the marks, you must state why this is an issue. We do not know if the linear relationship between homework time and test scores continues beyond 10 hours.

Therefore, the prediction is **unreliable** (or not valid).

questions.specification:

parts.content: Find the mean number of push-ups, $\bar{p}$.
parts.markscheme:

- Solve for $h$: $h=2(0.4 h-28)+80 \Rightarrow h=0.8 h+24 \Rightarrow 0.2 h=24 \Rightarrow h=120$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the mean number of push-ups: $p=0.4(120)-28 = 20$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the intersection property
steps.content: The intersection of the two regression lines always occurs at the mean point $(\bar{p}, \bar{h})$. To find the mean number of push-ups $\bar{p}$, we solve the system of two linear equations simultaneously.

steps.title: Substitute expression for h
steps.content:
Since we want to find $\bar{p}$ directly, we substitute the expression for $h$ from the first equation into the second equation.

Substitute $h = 2p + 80$ into $p = 0.4h - 28$:

$$p = 0.4(2p + 80) - 28$$

steps.title: Expand and simplify
steps.content:
Expand the brackets and simplify the constant terms:

$$p = 0.4(2p) + 0.4(80) - 28$$

$$p = 0.8p + 32 - 28$$

$$p = 0.8p + 4$$

steps.title: Solve for p
steps.content:
Subtract $0.8p$ from both sides to isolate $p$:

$$p - 0.8p = 4$$

$$0.2p = 4$$

$$p = \frac{4}{0.2}$$

$$p = 20$$

steps.title: Identify the key property
steps.content:
A fundamental property of regression lines in IB Statistics is that both the line of $y$ on $x$ and the line of $x$ on $y$ intersect at the mean point $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$.

In this question, the two lines will intersect at the coordinates $(\bar{p}, \bar{h})$. To find the mean number of push-ups $\bar{p}$, we need to find the intersection point of the two given equations.

steps.title: Prepare equations for graphing

The first equation is already in the correct form:

$$h = 2p + 80$$

The second equation needs to be rearranged:

$$p = 0.4h - 28$$

$$p + 28 = 0.4h$$

$$h = \frac{p + 28}{0.4}$$

So our two functions to plot are $Y_1 = 2x + 80$ and $Y_2 = \frac{x+28}{0.4}$.

parts.content: Identify which diagram shows (i) no correlation, (ii) strong positive correlation, (iii) strong negative correlation.

3 marks total

steps.title: Method: Visual Trend Analysis
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we will use **Visual Trend Analysis**. This involves looking at the pattern of the data points in each scatter diagram:

*   **Direction**: Does the trend go up (positive) or down (negative) as we move from left to right?
*   **Spread**: How closely do the points follow a line? Tighter clustering indicates a stronger correlation. If points are scattered randomly, there is no correlation.

steps.title: Analyze Diagram A
steps.content:
Looking at **Diagram A**, the data points are scattered randomly across the grid. There is no clear upward or downward trend, and the points do not cluster around any specific line.

This indicates **no correlation**.

steps.title: Analyze Diagram B
steps.content:
In **Diagram B**, notice that as the horizontal value ($x$) increases, the vertical value ($y$) also increases. The points form a very distinct pattern sloping **upwards** from left to right.

Since the points are tightly grouped in an upward direction, this represents a **strong positive correlation**.

steps.title: Analyze Diagram C
steps.content:
In **Diagram C**, as the horizontal value ($x$) increases, the vertical value ($y$) decreases. The points follow a clear path sloping **downwards** from left to right.

Because the points are tightly grouped in a downward direction, this represents a **strong negative correlation**.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on our analysis:

- (i) No correlation: **Diagram A**
- (ii) Strong positive correlation: **Diagram B**
- (iii) Strong negative correlation: **Diagram C**

parts.content: Given correlation coefficients: $0.95, -0.90, 0.10, -0.05, 1.20$, assign the appropriate coefficient to each diagram and explain why $1.20$ is rejected.
parts.markscheme:
- Assign coefficient to Diagram A: $-0.05$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Assign coefficient to Diagram B: $0.95$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Assign coefficient to Diagram C: $-0.90$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Explain why $1.20$ is rejected: Correlation coefficients must be between $-1$ and $1$ inclusive <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Recall correlation properties
steps.content:
First, recall that the Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient, $r$, measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship. The value of $r$ must always satisfy the condition:

$$-1 \le r \le 1$$

- Positive $r$ indicates an upward trend.
- Negative $r$ indicates a downward trend.
- Values close to $1$ or $-1$ indicate a strong relationship.
- Values close to $0$ indicate a weak or non-existent relationship.

steps.title: Reject the invalid value
steps.content:
Check the given list of coefficients: $0.95, -0.90, 0.10, -0.05, 1.20$. 

The value $1.20$ is greater than $1$, which violates the definition of the correlation coefficient.

Therefore, $1.20$ is the rejected value.

steps.title: Analyze Diagram B
steps.content:
Looking at **Diagram B**, the points are tightly clustered around a straight line that slopes **upwards** from left to right. This indicates a very strong positive correlation.

From the remaining valid options ($0.95, -0.90, 0.10, -0.05$), the value $0.95$ is the strongest positive coefficient.

**Assignment:** Diagram B corresponds to $0.95$.

steps.title: Analyze Diagram C
steps.content:
Looking at **Diagram C**, the points form a clear linear pattern that slopes **downwards** from left to right. This indicates a strong negative correlation.

From the remaining options ($-0.90, 0.10, -0.05$), the value $-0.90$ is the strongest negative coefficient.

**Assignment:** Diagram C corresponds to $-0.90$.

steps.title: Analyze Diagram A
steps.content:
Looking at **Diagram A**, the points are widely scattered with no obvious upward or downward trend. This indicates a very weak or non-existent correlation, meaning the coefficient should be closest to $0$.

The remaining options are $0.10$ and $-0.05$. Comparing their magnitudes (absolute values):

$$\begin{aligned} \lvert 0.10 \rvert &= 0.10 \\ \lvert -0.05 \rvert &= 0.05 \end{aligned}$$

Since $0.05 < 0.10$, the value $-0.05$ represents the weaker correlation, which best fits the random scatter in Diagram A.

**Assignment:** Diagram A corresponds to $-0.05$.

steps.title: Identify variables and formulas
steps.content:
We need to find the regression line of $h$ on $s$. The equation is given as $h = as + b$. This implies that $h$ is the dependent variable ($y$) and $s$ is the independent variable ($x$).

To find the slope $a$ and vertical intercept $b$ using regression formulas, we first need the mean of the scores $\bar{s}$ and the mean of the hours $\bar{h}$. Then we use the least squares formulas:

$$a = \frac{S_{sh}}{S_{ss}} = \frac{\sum(s_i - \bar{s})(h_i - \bar{h})}{\sum(s_i - \bar{s})^2}$$

$$b = \bar{h} - a\bar{s}$$

steps.title: Calculate the means
steps.content:
First, calculate the mean of the scores ($s$) and the hours ($h$).

$$\bar{s} = \frac{40+55+60+70+80+85}{6} = \frac{390}{6} = 65$$

$$\bar{h} = \frac{1+3+5+7+9+11}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the average of the s values and h values.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the average of the s values and h values.

calculator.description: Calculate the mean of s and h

steps.title: Calculate the slope a
steps.content:
To find the slope $a$, we calculate the sum of the products of differences (numerator) and the sum of squared differences for $s$ (denominator).

**Numerator ($S_{sh}$):**
$$\begin{aligned} \sum(s_i - \bar{s})(h_i - \bar{h}) &= (40-65)(1-6) + (55-65)(3-6) + \dots \\ &= (-25)(-5) + (-10)(-3) + (-5)(-1) \\ &\quad + (5)(1) + (15)(3) + (20)(5) \\ &= 125 + 30 + 5 + 5 + 45 + 100 \\ &= 310 \end{aligned}$$

**Denominator ($S_{ss}$):**
$$\begin{aligned} \sum(s_i - \bar{s})^2 &= (-25)^2 + (-10)^2 + (-5)^2 + (5)^2 + (15)^2 + (20)^2 \\ &= 625 + 100 + 25 + 25 + 225 + 400 \\ &= 1400 \end{aligned}$$

Now, calculate $a$:
$$a = \frac{310}{1400} \approx 0.221$$

gdc.instructions: Compute the sum of products and sum of squares.

ti84.instructions: Compute the sum of products and sum of squares.

desmos.instructions: Calculate 310/1400

calculator.description: Calculate the numerator and denominator sums

steps.title: Calculate the intercept b
steps.content:
Use the formula $b = \bar{h} - a\bar{s}$ to find the vertical intercept. We use the exact fraction for $a$ to maintain accuracy.

$$\begin{aligned} b &= 6 - \left(\frac{310}{1400}\right)(65) \\ &= 6 - \left(\frac{31}{140}\right)(65) \\ &\approx 6 - 14.393 \\ &\approx -8.39 \end{aligned}$$

So, the regression coefficients are:
$$a \approx 0.221, \quad b \approx -8.39$$

steps.title: State the value of r

From the calculator output, identify the value of $r$. Round the value to 3 significant figures.

$$r \approx -0.998$$

steps.title: Interpret the result
steps.content:
The question asks to interpret the value in context. We look at the magnitude (strength) and the sign (direction).

1.  **Strength:** Since $|r| \approx 0.998$ is very close to 1, there is a **strong** correlation.
2.  **Direction:** Since $r$ is negative, there is a **negative** correlation.
3.  **Context:** This means that **higher screen time is associated with a lower GPA**.

steps.title: Identify the variables

steps.content:

steps.title: State the equation
steps.content:
Round the values to 3 significant figures as per IB standards.

*   $a \approx -0.0498$
*   $b \approx 4.25$

Substitute these into the equation for the regression line of $p$ on $s$:

$$p = -0.0498s + 4.25$$

steps.title: Identify variables
steps.content:
First, we identify the independent and dependent variables from the question:
- Independent variable (predictor), $x = s$ (weekly screen time)
- Dependent variable (response), $y = p$ (academic performance/GPA)

We need to find the regression equation in the form $p = ms + c$ (or $p = as + b$).

steps.title: Calculate summary statistics
steps.content:
Using the calculator's statistics mode, enter the data for $s$ and $p$ into two lists to calculate the mean, standard deviation, and correlation coefficient.

**Means:**
$$\bar{s} \approx 27.33$$

$$\bar{p} \approx 2.892$$

**Standard Deviations:**
$$s_s \approx 13.10$$

$$s_p \approx 0.6529$$

**Correlation Coefficient:**
$$r \approx -0.9980$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Statistics mode.
2. Enter 's' values into List 1 and 'p' values into List 2.
3. Select Calc > Two-Variable.
4. Read values for mean, sx, sy, and r.

1. Press [STAT] > Edit.

5. For 'r', run Linear Regression (LinReg(ax+b)).

desmos.instructions:
1. Create a table with columns x1 (s) and y1 (p).
2. Type 'mean(x1)', 'stdev(x1)', 'mean(y1)', 'stdev(y1)', 'corr(x1, y1)' to find values.

calculator.description: Calculate summary statistics (means, standard deviations, and correlation)

steps.title: Calculate the gradient
steps.content:
The gradient (slope) $m$ of the regression line $y$ on $x$ is given by the formula:

$$m = r \frac{s_y}{s_x}$$

Substitute the calculated values:

$$m = -0.9980 \times \frac{0.6529}{13.10}$$

$$m \approx -0.0498$$

gdc.instructions: Compute the slope using the formula.

ti84.instructions: Compute the slope using the formula.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the expression.

steps.title: Calculate the intercept
steps.content:
The regression line passes through the mean point $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$. We use the equation of a line to find the y-intercept $c$:

$$c = \bar{y} - m\bar{x}$$

Substitute the values for $\bar{p}$, $m$, and $\bar{s}$:

$$c = 2.892 - (-0.0498)(27.33)$$

$$c \approx 2.892 + 1.361$$

$$c \approx 4.25$$

gdc.instructions: Compute the intercept using the formula.

ti84.instructions: Compute the intercept using the formula.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the expression.

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.content:
Substituting the gradient and intercept back into the equation $p = ms + c$, we get:

$$p = -0.0498s + 4.25$$

This matches the markscheme form.

parts.content: A stratified sample of 6 students is to be selected, proportional to screen time categories: low ($s < 20$ hours), medium ($20 \le s \le 35$ hours), and high ($s > 35$ hours). Determine the number of students from each category.
parts.markscheme:
Total students: 12. Categories based on data: Low (4), Medium (5), High (3).

- Calculate sample size for Low category: $\frac{4}{12} \times 6 = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate sample size for Medium category: $\frac{5}{12} \times 6 = 2.5 \rightarrow 3$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate sample size for High category: $\frac{3}{12} \times 6 = 1.5 \rightarrow 1$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Group data by category
steps.content:
First, we need to determine the stratum size (number of students) for each category based on the screen time $s$. We count the students from the given table for each group:

*   **Low** ($s < 20$): 10, 15, 12, 18 $\Rightarrow$ **4 students**
*   **Medium** ($20 \le s \le 35$): 20, 25, 30, 35, 28 $\Rightarrow$ **5 students**
*   **High** ($s > 35$): 40, 45, 50 $\Rightarrow$ **3 students**

Total population size = $4 + 5 + 3 = 12$.

steps.title: Calculate Low category sample
steps.content:
The formula for stratified sampling is:

$$ \text{Number in sample} = \frac{\text{Stratum size}}{\text{Total population}} \times \text{Sample size} $$

For the **Low** category:

$$ \frac{4}{12} \times 6 = 2 $$

So, we select **2 students** from the Low category.

steps.title: Finalize and round values
steps.content:
We have the calculated values: 2, 2.5, and 1.5. The sum is $2 + 2.5 + 1.5 = 6$, which matches our target sample size.

To get integer values, we round the non-integers. Since the Medium group is larger (5 students) than the High group (3 students), it is standard convention to round the larger stratum up.

*   **Low:** 2
*   **Medium:** $2.5 \rightarrow 3$
*   **High:** $1.5 \rightarrow 1$

**Final Answer:** 2 Low, 3 Medium, 1 High.

steps.title: Count students in each category
steps.content:
First, we categorize the 12 students based on their screen time $s$. We count how many fall into each group defined in the question:

*   **Low** ($s < 20$): The values are 10, 15, 12, 18. (Count: 4)
*   **Medium** ($20 \le s \le 35$): The values are 20, 25, 30, 35, 28. (Count: 5)
*   **High** ($s > 35$): The values are 40, 45, 50. (Count: 3)

Check that the total is correct: $$4 + 5 + 3 = 12$$

steps.title: Determine the scaling ratio
steps.content:
We need to select a sample of 6 students from a population of 12. Notice that the sample size is exactly half the population size:

$$ \text{Ratio} = \frac{\text{Sample Size}}{\text{Population Size}} = \frac{6}{12} = 0.5 $$

Using the **Scaling Ratio Approach**, we can simply divide the count of each category by 2 to find the number of students needed.

steps.title: Calculate Low category sample
steps.content:
For the **Low** category, we divide the count of 4 by 2:

$$ \frac{4}{2} = 2 $$

So, we select **2 students** from the Low category.

Since we cannot select half a student, we must round to a whole number. To ensure our total sample size is 6, we typically round the larger fraction up or adjust to fit the total. In this case, we round 2.5 up to **3 students**.

Looking at the table and scatter diagram, as the training hours ($t$) increase, the sprint time ($s$) decreases. The data points follow a clear straight-line pattern and are tightly clustered.

Therefore, the relationship is a **strong negative linear correlation**.

steps.title: Calculate mean training hours
steps.content:
To find the mean of $t$ (denoted as $\bar{t}$), sum all the training hours and divide by the number of athletes ($n=10$).

$$\bar{t} = \frac{\sum t}{n}$$

$$\bar{t} = \frac{5 + 8 + 10 + 12 + 14 + 16 + 18 + 20 + 22 + 25}{10}$$

$$\bar{t} = \frac{150}{10} = 15$$

steps.title: Calculate mean sprint times
steps.content:
Next, calculate the mean of $s$ (denoted as $\bar{s}$) by summing the sprint times and dividing by 10.

$$\bar{s} = \frac{\sum s}{n}$$

$$\bar{s} = \frac{12.9 + 12.3 + 12.2 + 12.1 + 11.5 + 11.2 + 11.3 + 10.9 + 10.4 + 10.2}{10}$$

$$\bar{s} = \frac{115}{10} = 11.5$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The coordinates of the mean point $M(\bar{t}, \bar{s})$ are:

$$M(15, 11.5)$$

steps.title: Method Explanation
steps.content:
To estimate a value using regression, we must use the appropriate regression line based on which variable is independent (known) and which is dependent (being estimated).

*   **To estimate sprint time ($s$)**: We treat $t$ as the independent variable. We use the regression line of **$s$ on $t$**.
*   **To estimate training hours ($t$)**: We treat $s$ as the independent variable. We use the regression line of **$t$ on $s$**.

This approach, using separate lines for each estimation, minimizes the error in the specific variable being predicted.

steps.content:
First, input the data into your calculator to find the regression equation of $s$ on $t$. Let $L_1$ be the training hours ($t$) and $L_2$ be the sprint times ($s$).

The linear regression equation is in the form $s = at + b$.

From the calculator:
$$a \approx -0.135$$
$$b \approx 13.5$$

So, the equation is:
$$s = -0.135t + 13.5$$

steps.title: Comment on validity
steps.content:
To assess validity, we check if the given values lie within the range of the original data (interpolation) or outside it (extrapolation).

*   **For $t=17$**: The data for training hours ranges from $5$ to $25$. Since $5 \le 17 \le 25$, this is **interpolation** and the estimate is reliable.
*   **For $s=11.3$**: The data for sprint times ranges from $10.2$ to $12.9$. Since $10.2 \le 11.3 \le 12.9$, this is **interpolation** and the estimate is reliable.

**Conclusion:** Both estimates are valid because they involve interpolation.

steps.title: Identify the regression lines needed
steps.content:
To estimate one variable given the other, we must use the appropriate regression line to minimize errors in the correct dimension:

*   To estimate sprint time $s$ given training hours $t$, use the regression line of **$s$ on $t$** ($s = at + b$).
*   To estimate training hours $t$ given sprint time $s$, use the regression line of **$t$ on $s$** ($t = cs + d$).

Using the wrong line (e.g., rearranging $s$ on $t$ to find $t$) is a common error and will lead to an incorrect answer.

steps.title: Strategy: Two-point method
steps.content: To draw the regression line $m = 1.5h + 35$, we need to find the coordinates of two points that lie on this line. We do this by choosing two convenient values for $h$ (revision hours) and calculating the corresponding $m$ (exam mark) for each.

steps.title: Calculate first point (h=0)
steps.content:
First, let's find the $y$-intercept by setting $h = 0$. We substitute this into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} m &= 1.5(0) + 35 \\ m &= 35 \end{aligned}$$

So, our first coordinate is $(0, 35)$.

steps.content:
Next, let's choose a value further along the $h$-axis to ensure our line is accurate. Let's use $h = 40$. Substitute this into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} m &= 1.5(40) + 35 \\ m &= 60 + 35 \\ m &= 95 \end{aligned}$$

So, our second coordinate is $(40, 95)$.

steps.title: Strategy
steps.content:
To draw a straight line on a scatter diagram, we need to find the coordinates of two points that lie on the line and connect them. 

The most efficient method for a regression line is to use:
1. The **mean point** $(\bar{h}, \bar{m})$, which the line must pass through.
2. The **vertical intercept** (where $h=0$) from the equation.

steps.title: Plot the mean point

A fundamental property of the regression line is that it passes through the mean point $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$. The question explicitly gives us these mean values.

We locate $h=20$ on the horizontal axis and $m=65$ on the vertical axis, then plot this point.

parts.content: Find the mean stress level and sleep duration.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to solve the system of equations formed by the two regression lines: $d=-0.5 s+9$ and $s=-1.6 d+15.2$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute $s=-1.6 d+15.2$ into $d=-0.5 s+9$:
$$d=-0.5(-1.6 d+15.2)+9 \Rightarrow d=0.8 d-7.6+9 \Rightarrow d=0.8 d+1.4$$
$$0.2 d=1.4 \Rightarrow d=7$$

- Calculate corresponding $s$ and state means:
$$\begin{aligned} s & =-1.6(7)+15.2=4 \\ \bar{s} & =4, \bar{d}=7 \end{aligned}$$ <marks>A1</marks> <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the intersection property
steps.content:
A key property of linear regression is that both the line of $d$ on $s$ and the line of $s$ on $d$ pass through the mean point $(\bar{s}, \bar{d})$.

Therefore, to find the mean stress level $\bar{s}$ and mean sleep duration $\bar{d}$, we must solve the system formed by the two given equations simultaneously.

steps.title: Substitute s into d equation
steps.content:
We are given two equations:

$$d = -0.5s + 9$$

$$s = -1.6d + 15.2$$

Using the **Algebraic Substitution** method, we substitute the entire expression for $s$ from the second equation directly into the first equation:

$$d = -0.5(-1.6d + 15.2) + 9$$

steps.title: Solve for d
steps.content:
Now we simplify the equation to solve for $d$. First, expand the brackets:

$$d = 0.8d - 7.6 + 9$$

Combine the constant terms:

$$d = 0.8d + 1.4$$

Subtract $0.8d$ from both sides:

$$0.2d = 1.4$$

Divide by $0.2$:

$$d = \frac{1.4}{0.2} = 7$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value of d

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value of d

desmos.instructions: Calculate the value of d

steps.title: Back-substitute to find s
steps.content:
Now substitute $d = 7$ back into the second equation to find $s$:

$$s = -1.6(7) + 15.2$$

$$s = -11.2 + 15.2$$

$$s = 4$$

gdc.instructions: Substitute d=7 into the equation for s

ti84.instructions: Substitute d=7 into the equation for s

desmos.instructions: Substitute d=7 into the equation for s

calculator.description: Calculate s using the found value of d

steps.title: State the final means
steps.content:
Since the intersection point represents the means, we have:

$$\bar{s} = 4$$

$$\bar{d} = 7$$

The mean stress level is $4$ and the mean sleep duration is $7$ hours.

steps.title: Recall regression line properties
steps.content:
A key property of linear regression is that both the line of $d$ on $s$ and the line of $s$ on $d$ pass through the mean point $(\bar{s}, \bar{d})$.

Therefore, to find the mean stress level and sleep duration, we simply need to find the intersection point of the two given lines by solving the system of equations.

steps.title: Rearrange the first equation
steps.content:
First, we take the regression line of $d$ on $s$ and rearrange it into the form $as + bd = c$ by moving the $s$ term to the left side.

$$d = -0.5s + 9$$

$$0.5s + d = 9$$

steps.title: Rearrange the second equation
steps.content:
Next, we rearrange the regression line of $s$ on $d$ into the same form by moving the $d$ term to the left side.

$$s = -1.6d + 15.2$$

$$s + 1.6d = 15.2$$

steps.title: Prepare for elimination
steps.content:
To eliminate the variable $s$, we can multiply the first rearranged equation by 2 so that the coefficient of $s$ becomes 1, matching the second equation.

$$2(0.5s + d) = 2(9)$$

$$s + 2d = 18$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 9 * 2

ti84.instructions: Calculate 9 * 2

desmos.instructions: Calculate 9 * 2

steps.title: Subtract to eliminate s
steps.content:
Now we have a system of two equations with the same $s$ coefficient:

1) $$s + 2d = 18$$

2) $$s + 1.6d = 15.2$$

Subtract equation (2) from equation (1) to eliminate $s$:

$$(s - s) + (2d - 1.6d) = 18 - 15.2$$

$$0.4d = 2.8$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 18 - 15.2

ti84.instructions: Calculate 18 - 15.2

desmos.instructions: Calculate 18 - 15.2

calculator.description: Subtract the constants to solve for d

steps.title: Solve for d
steps.content:
Divide by $0.4$ to find the mean sleep duration $\bar{d}$.

$$d = \frac{2.8}{0.4}$$

$$d = 7$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2.8 / 0.4

ti84.instructions: Calculate 2.8 / 0.4

desmos.instructions: Calculate 2.8 / 0.4

steps.title: Substitute to find s
steps.content:
Substitute $d = 7$ back into our first rearranged equation ($0.5s + d = 9$) to find the mean stress level $\bar{s}$.

$$0.5s + 7 = 9$$

$$0.5s = 2$$

$$s = 4$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The intersection point represents the means. 

Mean stress level: $\bar{s} = 4$

Mean sleep duration: $\bar{d} = 7$

steps.title: Identify the intersection property
steps.content:
A key property of linear regression is that both regression lines (line of $d$ on $s$ and line of $s$ on $d$) intersect at the point of the means $(\bar{s}, \bar{d})$.

To find the mean stress level and sleep duration, we simply need to find the coordinates where these two lines intersect.

steps.title: Prepare equations for graphing
steps.content:
To graph these on a calculator, we define $x = s$ (stress) and $y = d$ (sleep). We must express both equations in the form $y = \dots$

The first equation is already in the correct form:

$$y = -0.5x + 9$$

The second equation ($s = -1.6d + 15.2$) needs rearranging:

$$x = -1.6y + 15.2$$

$$1.6y = -x + 15.2$$

$$y = \frac{-x + 15.2}{1.6}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives the intersection point as $x = 4$ and $y = 7$. 

Since we defined $x = s$ and $y = d$, the mean values are:

$$\bar{s} = 4$$

$$\bar{d} = 7$$

parts.content: Calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient, $r$.
parts.markscheme:
- State relationship between slopes and correlation coefficient: $b_{d} \cdot b_{s}=r^{2}$

- Substitute slopes $-0.5$ and $-1.6$:
$$(-0.5)(-1.6)=0.8=r^{2} \Rightarrow r=\sqrt{0.8}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Determine sign and value (negative due to negative slopes):
$$r \approx -0.894$$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the regression slopes
steps.content:
First, we identify the gradients (slopes) from the two given regression line equations. The slope is the coefficient of the independent variable in each equation.

For the line of $d$ on $s$, the slope is $-0.5$. For the line of $s$ on $d$, the slope is $-1.6$.

steps.title: Apply product of slopes formula
steps.content:
Using the Product of Regression Slopes method, we know that the product of the two gradients equals the square of the Pearson correlation coefficient ($r^2$).

$$ r^2 = b_{d} \times b_{s} $$

steps.title: Calculate r squared
steps.content:
Substitute the slopes into the equation:

$$ r^2 = (-0.5) \times (-1.6) $$

$$ r^2 = 0.8 $$

gdc.instructions: Type -0.5 * -1.6 and press EXE

ti84.instructions: Type -0.5 * -1.6 and press ENTER

calculator.description: Calculate the product of the slopes

steps.title: Determine sign and final value
steps.content:
To find $r$, we take the square root of $0.8$. Since the regression slopes are negative, the correlation is negative, so we must take the negative root.

$$ r = -\sqrt{0.8} $$

$$ r \approx -0.894 $$

gdc.instructions: Type -sqrt(0.8) and press EXE

ti84.instructions: Press [(-)] [2nd] [x^2] (sqrt) 0.8 [ENTER]

desmos.instructions: Type -sqrt(0.8)

parts.content: An employee sleeps 5 hours. Estimate their stress level using both regression lines and explain the discrepancy.
parts.markscheme:
- Using $d=-0.5 s+9$: $5 = -0.5s + 9 \Rightarrow s = 8$. State that this line is not suitable for predicting $s$. <marks>A1</marks>

- Using $s=-1.6 d+15.2$: $s=-1.6(5)+15.2=7.2$. <marks>A1</marks>

- Discrepancy: Regression minimizes errors in the predicted variable; $s$ on $d$ is appropriate here. <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Draw both regression lines on the scatter diagram and identify their intersection.
parts.markscheme:

- Intersection at $(4, 7)$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the equations
steps.content:
To find the intersection point, we first identify the two regression equations given in the problem:

1.  Regression of $d$ on $s$: $$d = -0.5s + 9$$ 
2.  Regression of $s$ on $d$: $$s = -1.6d + 15.2$$ 

We will solve these simultaneous equations algebraically.

steps.title: Solve for s
steps.content:
Substitute the expression for $d$ from the first equation into the second equation to solve for $s$:

$$s = -1.6(-0.5s + 9) + 15.2$$

Expand the brackets:

$$s = 0.8s - 14.4 + 15.2$$

$$s = 0.8s + 0.8$$

Rearrange to solve for $s$:

$$0.2s = 0.8$$

$$s = \frac{0.8}{0.2} = 4$$

gdc.instructions: Type 0.8 / 0.2 and press EXE

ti84.instructions: Type 0.8 / 0.2 and press ENTER

steps.title: Solve for d
steps.content:
Now substitute $s = 4$ back into the first equation to find the value of $d$:

$$d = -0.5(4) + 9$$

$$d = -2 + 9$$

$$d = 7$$

Therefore, the exact intersection point is $(4, 7)$. This point represents the mean stress and mean sleep duration $(\bar{s}, \bar{d})$.

gdc.instructions: Type -0.5 * 4 + 9 and press EXE

ti84.instructions: Type -0.5 * 4 + 9 and press ENTER

steps.title: Plot the line of d on s
steps.content:
To draw the first line ($d = -0.5s + 9$), we need two points:
1.  The intersection point we just found: $(4, 7)$.
2.  The vertical intercept (when $s=0$): $$d = 9$$

Plot the point $(0, 9)$ on the vertical axis and draw a straight line passing through it and $(4, 7)$.

steps.title: Plot the line of s on d
steps.content:
To draw the second line ($s = -1.6d + 15.2$), we also use two points:

1. The intersection point: $(4, 7)$.
2. The vertical intercept (when $s=0$). Set $s=0$ and solve for $d$:

$$0 = -1.6d + 15.2$$

$$1.6d = 15.2$$

$$d = \frac{15.2}{1.6} = 9.5$$

Plot the point $(0, 9.5)$ on the vertical axis and draw a straight line passing through it and $(4, 7)$.

gdc.instructions: Type 15.2 / 1.6 and press EXE

ti84.instructions: Type 15.2 / 1.6 and press ENTER

steps.content:
Ensure both lines are drawn clearly on the diagram. Mark the point where they cross and label its coordinates.

The intersection is at **$(4, 7)$**.

The intersection of the two regression lines is the mean point $(\bar{s}, \bar{d})$. We can find this by solving the equations simultaneously or checking the intersection on your graph.

Let's check if the intersection point $(4, 7)$ works for both:

- Line 1: $7 = -0.5(4) + 9 \implies 7 = 7$ (Correct)

desmos.instructions: Click on the point where the two lines cross.

calculator.description: Find the intersection of the two lines

steps.title: Identify points to plot
steps.content:
To draw the lines accurately on paper, find two coordinates for each line using the Table view on your calculator.

**For Line 1 ($d = -0.5s + 9$):**
- At $s=4$, $d=7$ (Intersection)
- At $s=0$, $d=9$ (y-intercept)

**For Line 2 ($d = \frac{15.2-s}{1.6}$):**
- At $s=4$, $d=7$ (Intersection)
- At $s=12$, $d = \frac{15.2-12}{1.6} = \frac{3.2}{1.6} = 2$
- Or at $s=0$, $d = \frac{15.2}{1.6} = 9.5$

Draw straight lines through these pairs of points.

gdc.instructions: Go to Table menu, set range, and view table.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [GRAPH] (TABLE) to see x and y values for both equations.

desmos.instructions: Click the gear icon above the expression list and convert expressions to tables.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Draw the two lines on the scatter diagram using the points identified.

The intersection point is **$(4, 7)$**.

questions.specification:
A study records the amount of daily sleep, $t$ hours, and the reaction time, $r$ milliseconds, for eight participants. The following scatter plot shows the data collected.

![](https://files.prepable.com/679f7275d27863.09051862.png)

The regression line of $r$ on $t$ is $r = -40t + 460$. 
The regression line of $t$ on $r$ is $t = -0.02r + 10.65$.

parts.content: Estimate the reaction time for a participant who sleeps $7.2$ hours.
parts.markscheme:
Substitute $t = 7.2$ into the regression line of $r$ on $t$:  
$r = -40(7.2) + 460$ <marks>M1</marks>  
$r = -288 + 460$  
$r = 172$ ms <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the mean sleep time, $\bar{t}$, of the participants.
parts.markscheme:
The mean point $(\bar{t}, \bar{r})$ lies at the intersection of the two regression lines.  
Set up the system of equations:  
$r = -40t + 460$  
$t = -0.02r + 10.65$ <marks>M1</marks>  

Substitute the first equation into the second:  
$t = -0.02(-40t + 460) + 10.65$ <marks>M1</marks>  
$t = 0.8t - 9.2 + 10.65$  
$t = 0.8t + 1.45$  
$0.2t = 1.45$  
$t = 7.25$  

$\bar{t} = 7.25$ hours <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: On the grid provided in the specification, sketch the regression line of $r$ on $t$.
parts.markscheme:
![](https://files.prepable.com/679f7275e03eb0.89966133.png)

Line should pass through the mean point $(7.25, 170)$ and another point on the line, such as $(5, 260)$ or $(10, 60)$. <marks>A1</marks>  
Straight line drawn with negative gradient passing through the points. <marks>A1</marks>
